purpose: Demo checklist and Q&A prep for the Snail shell project.
important_notes: The shell supports built-ins, external commands, pipelines, input redirection, output redirection, append redirection, config loading from ~/snailShellrc, startup commands, persistent command history, tab completion, and dump-based safe deletion.
Restore logic exists in src/fileDump.c as fileRestore/fileRestoreHelper, but it does not appear to be exposed as a built-in shell command in executor.c or snailHelp.c.
A dumpList command header/source exists, but it also does not appear to be exposed as a built-in command in executor.c.
The mv command uses custom syntax: mv -re <old> <new>, mv -over <src> <dest>, mv -shift <src> <dir>.

features.title: Startup behavior and shell identity
features.why_it_matters: This is the fastest way to establish that Snail is a real custom shell with its own REPL, prompt, startup flow, config loading, and branding.
show_first: Start Snail normally.
Point out the custom prompt and the ASCII snail art if enabled.
Mention that startup commands from ~/snailShellrc can run automatically.
If configured, mention dump cleanup and clear-screen-on-start happen automatically.
good_commands_to_show: Run the shell and pause on first screen.
If startup commands exist in ~/snailShellrc, keep one simple command like echo Welcome to Snail.
edge_cases_or_questions: If asked what happens on first run: Snail creates ~/snailShellrc from snail.conf or built-in defaults.
If asked what happens when config creation fails: the code falls back to in-memory defaults and prints a warning.
If asked whether dump cleanup is automatic: yes, if clean_dump_on_start is enabled.

features.title: snailHelp built-in command
features.why_it_matters: This is the cleanest overview feature and also proves built-in command execution.
show_first: Run snailHelp.
Scroll to built-in commands, shell syntax, rm options, config path, dump folder, and prompt/config options.
Then show that built-in output can also be redirected.
edge_cases_or_questions: If asked where config is loaded from: snailHelp prints the active path from getSnailConfigPath().
If asked whether built-ins support redirection: yes, executor.c saves and restores stdin/stdout around built-in execution.

features.title: Built-in commands
features.why_it_matters: These show that the shell does more than just forward commands to execvp.
show_first: Use a short sequence such as pwd, mkdir, touch, ls, tree, cd, and clear.
Keep it in one temporary demo folder so the narrative stays clean.
edge_cases_or_questions: cd with no argument goes to HOME.
cd supports ~ and ~/path expansion.
cd with too many arguments prints an error.
mkdir with no operand prints an error.
touch with no file operand prints an error.
tree with too many arguments prints an error.
clear with extra arguments prints an error.

features.title: Custom mv command behavior
features.why_it_matters: This is one of the more custom parts of the shell and is easy for evaluators to ask about.
show_first: Explain that Snail intentionally does not use standard mv syntax.
Show rename first because it is easiest to understand.
Then show move-to-directory or overwrite behavior.
edge_cases_or_questions: If the option is missing or invalid, mv prints an error.
If the number of arguments is wrong, mv prints an error.
If overwrite or rename would replace an existing destination, the destination is first backed up to the dump.
If a move fails after backing up the destination, the code attempts rollback using restore helper logic.
Trying to rename over a directory is rejected.

features.title: External command execution
features.why_it_matters: This proves Snail can execute normal system programs and is not limited to built-ins.
show_first: Run one very familiar command such as pwd or echo through the system.
Then run something that clearly is not a Snail built-in, such as wc, cat, or grep.
edge_cases_or_questions: If the command does not exist, execvp fails and the shell prints an error.
External commands also participate in pipelines and redirection.

features.title: Input and output redirection
features.why_it_matters: This is a core shell capability and a common demo expectation.
show_first: Show output redirection with a simple command.
Then show append redirection.
Then show input redirection.
edge_cases_or_questions: Multiple output redirections in one command are rejected by the parser.
Multiple input redirections in one command are rejected by the parser.
Missing filename after <, >, or >> is rejected by the parser.
Built-ins also support redirection, not just external commands.
Output redirection uses truncate for > and append for >>.

features.title: Pipelines
features.why_it_matters: Pipelines are one of the strongest proofs of shell-like behavior.
show_first: Show a simple external-to-external pipeline first.
Then show a built-in participating in a pipeline.
edge_cases_or_questions: Invalid pipelines such as an empty stage are rejected.
Built-ins can run inside pipelines because executePipes uses executeBuiltIn in child processes.
Pipeline stages can still use input/output redirection because setupInputRedirection and setupOutputRedirection are called inside each child.

features.title: Safe deletion with rm, rmdir, and the dump
features.why_it_matters: This is one of Snail's standout differentiators from a normal shell.
show_first: Create a sample file and remove it with rm.
Immediately show that the file is gone from the working directory but present in ~/.snailDump/files with metadata in ~/.snailDump/info.
Then show directory handling with rm -r or rmdir.
good_commands_to_show: Navigate back manually to your demo folder
edge_cases_or_questions: rm without operands prints an error.
rm on a directory without -r is rejected.
rm -f ignores missing files if errno is ENOENT.
rm -i is a custom interactive mode for selecting files inside a directory; it cannot be combined with -r or -f.
rmdir only removes empty directories and sends them to the dump.
The dump cleanup policy removes entries older than 48 hours when enabled on startup.
The dump stores both file contents and metadata including original path, deletion date, and type.
Cross-device dump moves are not fully supported yet; the code prints a message if rename hits EXDEV.

features.title: Restoration logic and current limitation
features.why_it_matters: You specifically mentioned restoration, so it is best to present the exact state honestly.
show_first: Explain that restoration logic exists in the codebase and matches entries by original path metadata.
Mention that if multiple dumped versions of the same path exist, the newest matching one is chosen.
Be explicit that this restore path is currently implemented in fileDump.c but not exposed as a shell built-in command yet.
good_commands_to_show: Show the metadata files under ~/.snailDump/info and point out the stored original path and deletion date.
If asked for a live restore command, explain that the code support exists internally but is not currently wired into executor.c as a user-facing command.
edge_cases_or_questions: Restore refuses to overwrite an already existing destination.
Restore fails if the parent directory of the original path no longer exists.
Restore selects the newest matching dump entry for the same original path.
This is a good future-scope item: expose restore as a built-in command.

features.title: snailrc configuration file and live reload
features.why_it_matters: This shows customization, persistence, and a polished developer experience.
show_first: Open or briefly display ~/snailShellrc.
Point out theme, prompt, startup, ui, and shell sections.
Change one visible setting, then run reloadConfig to apply it without restarting.
edge_cases_or_questions: reloadConfig with extra arguments prints an error.
If reload fails, the shell prints an error and keeps running.
Booleans accept true/false, yes/no, on/off, and 1/0.
path_style supports full, home_relative, basename, and shortened.
Startup commands can be defined as command1, command2, command3, etc.

features.title: History, prompt interaction, and line editing
features.why_it_matters: These make the shell feel interactive and complete, not just parser plus exec.
show_first: Run a few commands.
Use up-arrow and down-arrow to navigate history.
Use left and right arrows to move within the line.
Show tab completion on a file or directory prefix.
good_commands_to_show: Type a few commands first, then use arrow keys.
Create similarly named files and press Tab to show completion or suggestions.
edge_cases_or_questions: History persists across sessions in ~/.snailCHistory.
Ctrl+L clears the screen and redraws the prompt.
Ctrl+D exits the shell.
Tab completion supports case-insensitive prefix matching.
If there are multiple matches with no longer common prefix, Snail lists candidates.

features.title: Extra features you may want to mention
features.why_it_matters: These are features evaluators often count as polish even if they were not explicitly requested.
show_first: Mention these after the main shell features so they feel like bonuses, not distractions.
good_commands_to_show: rm -i <directory> for interactive file selection inside a directory
edge_cases_or_questions: The prompt can show git branch, exit status, time, and last command duration depending on config.
ls output colors are configurable through the config file.
The shell measures last command duration and can display it when above a threshold.
Startup commands execute through the same parser and executor flow as normal commands.
Built-ins are added to history, and pipelines are stored as a combined history entry.

likely_questions_you_can_answer_confiden.question: What makes Snail different from just using /bin/sh or bash?
answer_points: It is a custom shell written in C with its own parser, REPL, built-ins, prompt system, config loader, history, tab completion, and safe deletion workflow.
Its standout custom behavior is dump-based deletion instead of immediate permanent removal.

likely_questions_you_can_answer_confiden.question: Do built-ins also work with redirection and pipelines?
answer_points: For normal execution, executor.c temporarily redirects stdin/stdout around built-ins.
For pipelines, built-ins run in child processes through executeBuiltIn.

likely_questions_you_can_answer_confiden.question: How is deletion made safer?
answer_points: rm and rmdir send entries to ~/.snailDump instead of permanently deleting them.
Metadata records the original path, deletion time, and type.
Old dump entries can be cleaned automatically after 48 hours.

likely_questions_you_can_answer_confiden.question: Can deleted files be restored?
answer_points: The restore logic exists internally in src/fileDump.c.
It restores by original path metadata and picks the newest match.
At the moment it is not yet exposed as a shell command, so that is a current limitation or future enhancement.

likely_questions_you_can_answer_confiden.question: How do you handle invalid syntax?
answer_points: The parser rejects malformed redirection and invalid pipelines.
Individual commands also validate operands and options and return errors clearly.

demo_tips: Keep one dedicated demo folder so you can create and remove files without clutter.
Prefer short commands with visible output.
For the dump demo, create tiny throwaway files so the before-and-after is obvious.
If someone asks for restore live, present it as implemented backend logic plus a currently missing command hook, not as a failed feature.
